texts.orig: OPTIMAL BUBBLE RIDING: A MEAN FIELD GAME WITH VARYING ENTRY TIMES
texts.text: OPTIMAL BUBBLE RIDING: A MEAN FIELD GAME WITH VARYING ENTRY TIMES

texts.label: text
texts.orig: LUDOVIC TANGPI AND SHICHUN WANG
texts.text: LUDOVIC TANGPI AND SHICHUN WANG

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: February 2, 2024
texts.text: February 2, 2024

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. Recent financial bubbles such as the emergence of cryptocurrencies and 'meme stocks' have gained increasing attention from both retail and institutional investors. In this paper, we propose a game-theoretic model on optimal liquidation in the presence of an asset bubble. Our setup allows the influx of players to fuel the price of the asset. Moreover, traders enter the market at possibly different times and take advantage of the uptrend at the risk of an inevitable crash. In particular, we consider two types of crashes: an endogenous burst which results from excessive selling, and an exogenous burst which cannot be anticipated and is independent from the actions of the traders.
texts.text: Abstract. Recent financial bubbles such as the emergence of cryptocurrencies and 'meme stocks' have gained increasing attention from both retail and institutional investors. In this paper, we propose a game-theoretic model on optimal liquidation in the presence of an asset bubble. Our setup allows the influx of players to fuel the price of the asset. Moreover, traders enter the market at possibly different times and take advantage of the uptrend at the risk of an inevitable crash. In particular, we consider two types of crashes: an endogenous burst which results from excessive selling, and an exogenous burst which cannot be anticipated and is independent from the actions of the traders.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The popularity of asset bubbles suggests a large-population setting, which naturally leads to a mean field game (MFG) formulation. We introduce a class of MFGs with varying entry times. In particular, an equilibrium depends on the entry-weighted average of conditional optimal strategies. To incorporate the exogenous burst time, we adopt the method of progressive enlargement of filtrations. We prove existence of MFG equilibria using the weak formulation in a generalized setup, and we show that the equilibrium strategy can be decomposed into before-and-after-burst segments, each part containing only the market information. We also perform numerical simulations of the solution, which allow us to provide some intriguing results on the relationship between the bubble burst and equilibrium strategies.
texts.text: The popularity of asset bubbles suggests a large-population setting, which naturally leads to a mean field game (MFG) formulation. We introduce a class of MFGs with varying entry times. In particular, an equilibrium depends on the entry-weighted average of conditional optimal strategies. To incorporate the exogenous burst time, we adopt the method of progressive enlargement of filtrations. We prove existence of MFG equilibria using the weak formulation in a generalized setup, and we show that the equilibrium strategy can be decomposed into before-and-after-burst segments, each part containing only the market information. We also perform numerical simulations of the solution, which allow us to provide some intriguing results on the relationship between the bubble burst and equilibrium strategies.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Asset bubbles from various financial markets have recently gained much attention. For instance, in 2021 the short squeeze on Reddit triggered a so-called 'meme stock' frenzy, whose impact surprised the financial industry by its unprecedented magnitude. Certain cryptocurrencies also experienced explosive price increase in the past decade. In particular, the price of Bitcoin grew 25 times in 2017, a trend reminiscent of the tech boom (also known as the 'dot-com' bubble) at the turn of the last century, during which the Nasdaq index rose 400%. Bubbles of this size can be detected [34, 41, 58, 56] and are attractive to traders despite the risk from an inevitable future price correction. In this paper, we shall analyze the notion of optimal bubble riding in a symmetric, large population game setting.
texts.text: Asset bubbles from various financial markets have recently gained much attention. For instance, in 2021 the short squeeze on Reddit triggered a so-called 'meme stock' frenzy, whose impact surprised the financial industry by its unprecedented magnitude. Certain cryptocurrencies also experienced explosive price increase in the past decade. In particular, the price of Bitcoin grew 25 times in 2017, a trend reminiscent of the tech boom (also known as the 'dot-com' bubble) at the turn of the last century, during which the Nasdaq index rose 400%. Bubbles of this size can be detected [34, 41, 58, 56] and are attractive to traders despite the risk from an inevitable future price correction. In this paper, we shall analyze the notion of optimal bubble riding in a symmetric, large population game setting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The soaring prices certainly attract non-institutional traders. Retail investors took up nearly 25% of U.S. equity trading volume in Q1 2021, compared to 15% in 2019 [24]. A similar trend was observed during the dot-com bubble, when a large wave of retail investors poured into tech stocks. What is puzzling, however, is that sophisticated investors such as hedge funds were also riding the dot-com bubble, despite anticipating a major price correction (see e.g. [46, 35] for empirical evidence). Such evidence challenges the traditional perspective which considers bubble riding merely as 'irrational exuberance' that results from the herding behavior of uninformed investors. Researchers have thus been interested in understanding rational bubble modeling.

Such evidence challenges the traditional perspective which considers bubble riding merely as 'irrational exuberance' that results from the herding behavior of uninformed investors. Researchers have thus been interested in understanding rational bubble modeling.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: OPTIMAL BUBBLE RIDING
texts.text: OPTIMAL BUBBLE RIDING

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among the most influential works on the topic, the theoretical model by Abreu and Brunnermeier [1] on profitable bubble riding offers insight to the persistence of asset bubbles in the presence of rational arbitrageurs (see also [29, 30] for extensions of this model). Sotes-Paladino and Zapatero [69] use a dynamic trading model to show that sophisticated, risk-averse money managers can invest in overvalued non-benchmark asset. The Log-Periodic Power Law (LPPL) model [44, 45, 66, 43], sometimes referred to as the Johansen-Ledoit-Sornette (JLS) model [68], characterizes bubbles by faster-than-exponential growth of the asset price with a finite singularity point that results from the positive feedback loop from investors. A more mathematical finance perspective is developed by Jarrow et al. [42] and Protter [63], who model bubbles using the theory of strict local martingales and various no-arbitrage arguments. Nonetheless, these models do not link the growth of the bubble directly to the inflow of traders, nor do they take advantage of the large population feature. Moreover, to our knowledge, the present paper is the first that looks into optimal bubble riding in a stochastic, non-cooperative, and continuous-time game perspective.
texts.text: Among the most influential works on the topic, the theoretical model by Abreu and Brunnermeier [1] on profitable bubble riding offers insight to the persistence of asset bubbles in the presence of rational arbitrageurs (see also [29, 30] for extensions of this model). Sotes-Paladino and Zapatero [69] use a dynamic trading model to show that sophisticated, risk-averse money managers can invest in overvalued non-benchmark asset. The Log-Periodic Power Law (LPPL) model [44, 45, 66, 43], sometimes referred to as the Johansen-Ledoit-Sornette (JLS) model [68], characterizes bubbles by faster-than-exponential growth of the asset price with a finite singularity point that results from the positive feedback loop from investors. A more mathematical finance perspective is developed by Jarrow et al. [42] and Protter [63], who model bubbles using the theory of strict local martingales and various no-arbitrage arguments. Nonetheless, these models do not link the growth of the bubble directly to the inflow of traders, nor do they take advantage of the large population feature. Moreover, to our knowledge, the present paper is the first that looks into optimal bubble riding in a stochastic, non-cooperative, and continuous-time game perspective.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In contrast to the works mentioned above, we integrate the size and the dynamics of the population into our model. Our basic framework borrows from the seminal work of Almgren and Chriss [4] on price impact and its multiplayer game extension by Carlin et al. [15]. The core of the price impact model is an optimal execution problem, where traders need to liquidate their assets in a given interval of time. This framework is suitable for bubble riding as well because most 'riders' simply wish to take advantage of the growth, and thus they do not intend to hold the overpriced asset. In an optimal liquidation setting, traders optimize their trading speed α to minimize the temporary trading cost while lowering their inventory. The existence of an asset bubble intensifies the urgency in light of the inevitable yet unpredictable moment when it will burst. The asset bubble's presence amplifies the urgency to liquidate, given the impending but uncertain moment of its burst. At the same time, however, the longer the trader holds onto the asset, the more she benefits from the growth of the bubble, provided that she pulls out before the crash.

This framework is suitable for bubble riding as well because most 'riders' simply wish to take advantage of the growth, and thus they do not intend to hold the overpriced asset. In an optimal liquidation setting, traders optimize their trading speed α to minimize the temporary trading cost while lowering their inventory. The existence of an asset bubble intensifies the urgency in light of the inevitable yet unpredictable moment when it will burst. The asset bubble's presence amplifies the urgency to liquidate, given the impending but uncertain moment of its burst. At the same time, however, the longer the trader holds onto the asset, the more she benefits from the growth of the bubble, provided that she pulls out before the crash.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are three main features of our model on top of the optimal execution framework: the varying entry times, a bubble component fueled by the influx of players, and a random burst time. Inspired by what Abreu and Brunnermeier [1] refer to as the 'awareness window', we fix some η > 0 that measures the level of 'heterogeneity' between traders, that is, how spread-out their entry times are. The bubble starts at time t = 0, and the players begin trading at various entry times in the interval [0 , η ]. There are two potential reasons to the delay of entry. The first one is information asymmetry among the traders. It is possible that the mispricing has not yet caught some traders' attention, or that some of them have insider information and begin trading earlier than others. Another reason, perhaps more interesting, is related to risk preferences. Some traders might not yet believe in the strength of the bubble, so they choose to wait longer before entering the game. For example, using data on brokerage accounts, Hasso et al. [36] found that investors who joined the GameStop frenzy earlier had a history of investing in 'lottery-like' stocks. However, since we are more interested in the Nash equilibrium and the group behavior, we consider the exact reason of entry as an extrinsic factor. In the setup of Abreu and Brunnermeier [1], players enter uniformly on [0 , η ]. Here we only assume that the entry times are independent and identically distributed (iid) with a fixed distribution known to all players. The traders do not optimize their strategies over entry times. In fact, as soon as the agent decides that the bubble is worth riding, the optimal action is to enter immediately. The decision to enter should thus depend on the traders' awareness of the existence of the bubble and her confidence in its strength. In our accompanying paper [70], for example, we use the price of the bubble asset as a proxy for these two factors at the expense of a weaker result and a more abstract proof. We do not model these two factors explicitly in this paper Our model emulates the five stages (Displacement, Boom, Euphoria, Profit-taking, and Panic) of a typical bubble identified by Minsky [57] and detailed in the book by Aliber and Kindleberger [2]. In particular, the Boom phase describes the period when the price increases due to waves of traders coming into the market. New players that join the game should boost the price differently from the standard market impact model. Therefore, we assume the price dynamics of the asset before the burst to have a bubble component plus a fundamental (or non-bubble) component; see (3). Specifically, the bubble component depends on the number of players in the game. As a result, a trader could have a large relative disadvantage even if she joins only slightly after a big crowd does. After the crash, the price dynamics loses the bubble component, and the model reduces to a standard optimal execution problem.

texts.orig: OPTIMAL BUBBLE RIDING
texts.text: OPTIMAL BUBBLE RIDING

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regarding the burst of the bubble, it is commonly believed that there are two types of market crashes: endogenous and exogenous. An endogenous burst (sometimes called a Minsky moment) refers to the collapse caused by the cumulative selling pressure. For instance, Griffin et al. [35] showed, using individual stock peaks during the dot-com bubble, that institutions began to sell rapidly on the day of the peak which triggered the drop in prices. In contrast to a trader-driven burst, an exogenous crash refers to a commonly observable but unanticipated event from outside the system that causes the price to plummet. A recent example took place in Q1 2020 when the COVID-19 pandemic triggered the so-called Coronavirus crash. The fact that it was an exogenous event is confirmed by Song et al. [65], in which the authors find no indication of endogenous factors behind the crash. Mirroring the approach of Abreu and Brunnermeier [1], we allow for both types of bursts. Specifically, the true crash occurs at the minimum of the two burst times and leads to a sudden drop in price. The size of the drop does not depend on the exact type of the burst.

An endogenous burst (sometimes called a Minsky moment) refers to the collapse caused by the cumulative selling pressure. For instance, Griffin et al. [35] showed, using individual stock peaks during the dot-com bubble, that institutions began to sell rapidly on the day of the peak which triggered the drop in prices. In contrast to a trader-driven burst, an exogenous crash refers to a commonly observable but unanticipated event from outside the system that causes the price to plummet. A recent example took place in Q1 2020 when the COVID-19 pandemic triggered the so-called Coronavirus crash. The fact that it was an exogenous event is confirmed by Song et al. [65], in which the authors find no indication of endogenous factors behind the crash. Mirroring the approach of Abreu and Brunnermeier [1], we allow for both types of bursts. Specifically, the true crash occurs at the minimum of the two burst times and leads to a sudden drop in price. The size of the drop does not depend on the exact type of the burst.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We define the endogenous burst time as the first time that the average inventory among the traders hits a given threshold level. The exogenous burst time is modeled as a random time, but not necessarily a stopping time in the underlying market filtration. We allow investors to continue trading after the burst until some fixed horizon. Each player chooses her continuous liquidation strategy to minimize the total cost (16) up to time T . As everyone's strategies should depend on the price, hence also the crash, the endogenous burst time couples all the players through their inventory levels. Similarly, the price impact term depends on the strategies of all the players. These complications make the game mathematically intractable to analyze, especially when the number of players gets large. A well-known workaround is to look at its mean field game (MFG) counterpart (see e.g. [13, 40, 23]).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Originally introduced by Lasry and Lions [53, 51, 52] and, independently, by Huang et al. [38, 39], MFGs are heuristic infinite population limits of symmetric stochastic differential games; see e.g. [26, 54, 50, 14] for rigorous proofs of the convergence of finite population games to MFG in various settings. As the number of players approaches infinity, the interdependence weakens due to the symmetry among the players. Instead of reacting to other players' actions, each player responds to a fixed distribution, and the equilibrium translates into a fixed point condition. We refer the readers to the monographs of Carmona and Delarue [17, 18] for a more detailed discussion on MFGs and additional references. We adopt the weak formulation approach proposed by Carmona and Lacker [19].
texts.text: Originally introduced by Lasry and Lions [53, 51, 52] and, independently, by Huang et al. [38, 39], MFGs are heuristic infinite population limits of symmetric stochastic differential games; see e.g. [26, 54, 50, 14] for rigorous proofs of the convergence of finite population games to MFG in various settings. As the number of players approaches infinity, the interdependence weakens due to the symmetry among the players. Instead of reacting to other players' actions, each player responds to a fixed distribution, and the equilibrium translates into a fixed point condition. We refer the readers to the monographs of Carmona and Delarue [17, 18] for a more detailed discussion on MFGs and additional references. We adopt the weak formulation approach proposed by Carmona and Lacker [19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The MFG that naturally emerges from our model has two distinct features which have been studied in the MFG literature. The first one is the notion of varying entry times. Suppose the representative player joins the game at time t ∗ ∈ [0 , η ]. Then her strategy and state processes are both kept at 0 before t ∗ . In a classical MFG, an equilibrium requires the laws of the optimal controls and state processes coincide with the distribution of that of all players. But if players enter the game at different times, clearly their controls, or inventory levels, cannot share a common law. Both the control and state variables are fixed at 0 for someone who has not yet entered the game. Therefore, the correct notion of consistency should aggregate the players by marginalizing the conditional laws over entry times. Specifically, the fixed point condition in Definition 5.2 is imposed on the marginal law by integrating the regular conditional probabilities. In other words, MFG equilibrium is defined as an entry-weighted average of optimal strategies.
texts.text: The MFG that naturally emerges from our model has two distinct features which have been studied in the MFG literature. The first one is the notion of varying entry times. Suppose the representative player joins the game at time t ∗ ∈ [0 , η ]. Then her strategy and state processes are both kept at 0 before t ∗ . In a classical MFG, an equilibrium requires the laws of the optimal controls and state processes coincide with the distribution of that of all players. But if players enter the game at different times, clearly their controls, or inventory levels, cannot share a common law. Both the control and state variables are fixed at 0 for someone who has not yet entered the game. Therefore, the correct notion of consistency should aggregate the players by marginalizing the conditional laws over entry times. Specifically, the fixed point condition in Definition 5.2 is imposed on the marginal law by integrating the regular conditional probabilities. In other words, MFG equilibrium is defined as an entry-weighted average of optimal strategies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another feature of our MFG comes from the exogenous burst time τ . As mentioned earlier, τ should be an unanticipated but observable event, which bears resemblance to default times in the credit risk literature [9]. To put it in mathematical terms, we are looking for a totally-inaccessible stopping time. However, such stopping times do not exist with respect to the natural choice of filtration which is generated by a Brownian motion. We will therefore solve the MFG on an enlarged filtration by adopting the method of progressive enlargement, a common technique used in credit risk analysis [72, 73]. On this new filtration, we can make an arbitrary random time on the original probability space into a totally inaccessible stopping time. However, we need to bring the solution back to the original filtration so that the optimal strategies of the traders still only depend on the available market information. This is achieved by adopting results of Kharroubi and Lim [49], Ankirchner et al. [5], and Pham [60].
texts.text: Another feature of our MFG comes from the exogenous burst time τ . As mentioned earlier, τ should be an unanticipated but observable event, which bears resemblance to default times in the credit risk literature [9]. To put it in mathematical terms, we are looking for a totally-inaccessible stopping time. However, such stopping times do not exist with respect to the natural choice of filtration which is generated by a Brownian motion. We will therefore solve the MFG on an enlarged filtration by adopting the method of progressive enlargement, a common technique used in credit risk analysis [72, 73]. On this new filtration, we can make an arbitrary random time on the original probability space into a totally inaccessible stopping time. However, we need to bring the solution back to the original filtration so that the optimal strategies of the traders still only depend on the available market information. This is achieved by adopting results of Kharroubi and Lim [49], Ankirchner et al. [5], and Pham [60].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we first prove the existence of MFG equilibrium (Theorem 3.2) for fixed entry time using Brouwer-Schauder-Tychonoff fixed point theorem. This approach should be compared to Carmona and Lacker [19] and Campi and Fischer [12]. We solve the MFG on the progressively enlarged filtration and decompose the equilibrium strategy into before-and-after-burst segments. Moreover, each part is progressively measurable with respect to the original filtration. Then, we prove the existence of MFG equilibrium with varying entry times (Theorem 5.3) through the perspective of probability kernels. Since the game introduced here is apparently new, we prove the existence results in a more generic formulation for mathematical completeness, which we then apply to the bubble riding game.

We solve the MFG on the progressively enlarged filtration and decompose the equilibrium strategy into before-and-after-burst segments. Moreover, each part is progressively measurable with respect to the original filtration. Then, we prove the existence of MFG equilibrium with varying entry times (Theorem 5.3) through the perspective of probability kernels. Since the game introduced here is apparently new, we prove the existence results in a more generic formulation for mathematical completeness, which we then apply to the bubble riding game.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The paper is structured as follows. In Section 2, we describe the N -player model for bubble riding as well as its limiting MFG. We then proceed to discuss the price dynamics and the bursting mechanism. Section 3 describes the general setup under filtration enlargement. We state the assumptions and the main existence result for fixed entry time and prove it in Section 4. Section 5 deals with varying entry times, and we prove the existence result in that setting. Finally in Section 6, we revisit the bubble riding problem and prove the existence of MFG equilibrium for the model, and we provide a discussion of our numerical simulation results. We conclude by giving an outline of the proof for using MFG equilibrium to construct approximate Nash equilibrium of the finite player game. Some proofs of technical results on progressive enlargement of filtration are included in the Appendix A.
texts.text: The paper is structured as follows. In Section 2, we describe the N -player model for bubble riding as well as its limiting MFG. We then proceed to discuss the price dynamics and the bursting mechanism. Section 3 describes the general setup under filtration enlargement. We state the assumptions and the main existence result for fixed entry time and prove it in Section 4. Section 5 deals with varying entry times, and we prove the existence result in that setting. Finally in Section 6, we revisit the bubble riding problem and prove the existence of MFG equilibrium for the model, and we provide a discussion of our numerical simulation results. We conclude by giving an outline of the proof for using MFG equilibrium to construct approximate Nash equilibrium of the finite player game. Some proofs of technical results on progressive enlargement of filtration are included in the Appendix A.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. The Bubble Riding Model and First Main Results
texts.text: 2. The Bubble Riding Model and First Main Results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1. The N-Player Game. A crowd of N agents trade on the same asset that is believed to be overpriced and has a persistent upward trend, resembling a bubble structure. The bubble starts at time t = 0, but the players have not necessarily started trading. We assume that the players begin trading at times independent from each other after the bubble starts. There could be two potential reasons to this delay of entry. It is possible that the mispricing has not yet caught the trader's attention, or the trader does not yet believe in the strength of the bubble. We make a simplifying assumption that the entry time is independent of everything else; though the second scenario suggests a price-dependent entry time, a case explored in our accompanying paper [70].
texts.text: 2.1. The N-Player Game. A crowd of N agents trade on the same asset that is believed to be overpriced and has a persistent upward trend, resembling a bubble structure. The bubble starts at time t = 0, but the players have not necessarily started trading. We assume that the players begin trading at times independent from each other after the bubble starts. There could be two potential reasons to this delay of entry. It is possible that the mispricing has not yet caught the trader's attention, or the trader does not yet believe in the strength of the bubble. We make a simplifying assumption that the entry time is independent of everything else; though the second scenario suggests a price-dependent entry time, a case explored in our accompanying paper [70].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1.1. Entry Time. We let η > 0 be a known parameter that measures the level of 'heterogeneity' between the N traders. The players begin trading at various entry times in the interval [0 , η ], referred to as the 'awareness window' by Abreu and Brunnermeier [1]. Let T = ( T 1 , . . . , T N ) ∼ ν N be a vector of i.i.d entry times, each with a fixed probability measure ν on [0 , η ] with cumulative distribution function F T . This distribution reflects the differences among traders in terms of their information and beliefs on the underlying asset. We impose the assumption that F T (0) > 0. In other words, we assume that there are people who are already actively trading before the bubble starts, or they might have some information to anticipate an upcoming upward trend. Let F N T denote the empirical CDF of the N entry times. Conditioning on T = t = ( t 1 , . . . , t N ), at each time t , the number of players already in the game is
texts.text: 2.1.1. Entry Time. We let η > 0 be a known parameter that measures the level of 'heterogeneity' between the N traders. The players begin trading at various entry times in the interval [0 , η ], referred to as the 'awareness window' by Abreu and Brunnermeier [1]. Let T = ( T 1 , . . . , T N ) ∼ ν N be a vector of i.i.d entry times, each with a fixed probability measure ν on [0 , η ] with cumulative distribution function F T . This distribution reflects the differences among traders in terms of their information and beliefs on the underlying asset. We impose the assumption that F T (0) > 0. In other words, we assume that there are people who are already actively trading before the bubble starts, or they might have some information to anticipate an upcoming upward trend. texts.text: 2.1.2. Trading Rate and Inventory. Let λ 0 denote some fixed initial probability measure on R + with finite second moment and positive first moment. At time t i , player i has an initial inventory X i t i = ι i i.i.d ∼ λ 0 . Suppose that there is a common horizon T ≥ η > 0, regardless of whether the bubble has burst or not. By choosing her trading rate α i = ( α i t ) t i ≤ t ≤ T , the player can control her inventory trajectory by

texts.label: formula

A constant σ > 0 is assumed for simplicity, which can be somewhat generalized (see Section 3). A positive α t corresponds to buying, and a negative α t corresponds to selling. Obviously, the total supply of this stock is finite. For other practical reasons such as transaction fees, we assume that at any time a player's trading rate is in a compact interval A ⊂ R . Before entry, naturally the trader has 0 inventory and does not have control over it. Hence we require α i t = 0 on t < t i for each i ∈ { 1 , . . . , N } .

texts.orig: 2.1.3. Permanent Price Impact. Trades convey information which has a long-term impact on the price dynamics. We use the N -player extension of the celebrated price impact model developed by Almgren and Chriss [4], where the instantaneous price impact depends on the trading rate. The impact function is usually assumed to be concave, a fact repeatedly supported by strong empirical evidence and theoretical models [11, 61]. Let ρ : A → R be a locally bounded function for the instantaneous impact with ρ (0) = 0. The true impact on the price is the average impact among the traders who have entered the game, which is defined as

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1.5. Bubble Burst Time. It is commonly believed that there are two types of mechanisms that cause the burst of a bubble: endogenous and exogenous. An endogenous burst refers to the collapse caused by the cumulative selling pressure, as modeled by Abreu and Brunnermeier in [1].
texts.text: 2.1.5. Bubble Burst Time. It is commonly believed that there are two types of mechanisms that cause the burst of a bubble: endogenous and exogenous. An endogenous burst refers to the collapse caused by the cumulative selling pressure, as modeled by Abreu and Brunnermeier in [1].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.3 ( N -Player endogenous burst time) . We define the N -player endogenous burst time as
texts.text: Definition 2.3 ( N -Player endogenous burst time) . We define the N -player endogenous burst time as

texts.label: formula

texts.text: where ζ is a deterministic, continuous and strictly increasing function of t with ζ 0 ∈ (0 , E [ ι ]) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The initial threshold ζ 0 should take up most of the threshold value. It is strictly positive to ensure the inevitability of the burst as the average inventory approaches 0. We require ζ 0 < E [ ι ] because the bubble should not burst the moment it starts. We explain in the remarks below the two properties of the endogenous burst time that follow from the definition.
texts.text: The initial threshold ζ 0 should take up most of the threshold value. It is strictly positive to ensure the inevitability of the burst as the average inventory approaches 0. We require ζ 0 < E [ ι ] because the bubble should not burst the moment it starts. We explain in the remarks below the two properties of the endogenous burst time that follow from the definition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 2.4 (Increasing fragility) . The system should become increasingly vulnerable to extensive selling as the bubble grows, so we require the threshold to be strictly increasing. We also use the running minimum of ¯ µ N , instead of the mean trajectory itself, to allow the market to 'remember' the time when buying pressure is at its lowest, enabling it to react to the bubble based on the weakest moment. On the other hand, we expect that in most scenarios the average inventory will eventually become monotone decreasing due to the pressure of the burst and terminal cost, which we discuss in the next section. For a more detailed discussion on the monotonicity of average inventory, see [13].
texts.text: Remark 2.4 (Increasing fragility) . The system should become increasingly vulnerable to extensive selling as the bubble grows, so we require the threshold to be strictly increasing. We also use the running minimum of ¯ µ N , instead of the mean trajectory itself, to allow the market to 'remember' the time when buying pressure is at its lowest, enabling it to react to the bubble based on the weakest moment. On the other hand, we expect that in most scenarios the average inventory will eventually become monotone decreasing due to the pressure of the burst and terminal cost, which we discuss in the next section. For a more detailed discussion on the monotonicity of average inventory, see [13].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 2.5 (Burst by T) . Notice that by this definition, the bubble will always burst at or before T . The model does not say anything about the asset after the burst. As such, our model is similar to the classical JLS bubble model [44, 45, 66, 67], in which a critical point marks the end of the bubble and potentially a transition point to a new regime. Also, requiring the bubble to burst endogenously by T is not too restrictive in the present setting. In fact, we impose a terminal cost that encourages liquidation before T , which by definition triggers the endogenous burst.
texts.text: Remark 2.5 (Burst by T) . Notice that by this definition, the bubble will always burst at or before T . The model does not say anything about the asset after the burst. As such, our model is similar to the classical JLS bubble model [44, 45, 66, 67], in which a critical point marks the end of the bubble and potentially a transition point to a new regime. Also, requiring the bubble to burst endogenously by T is not too restrictive in the present setting. In fact, we impose a terminal cost that encourages liquidation before T , which by definition triggers the endogenous burst.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An exogenous burst, sometimes called a sunspot event, refers to a commonly observable event that triggers the cascading price adjustments from outside the system. We model it by a random time τ on R + with distribution ν τ that is independent from the rest of the model. In other words, there is no information within the system that can help anticipate this event.
texts.text: An exogenous burst, sometimes called a sunspot event, refers to a commonly observable event that triggers the cascading price adjustments from outside the system. We model it by a random time τ on R + with distribution ν τ that is independent from the rest of the model. In other words, there is no information within the system that can help anticipate this event.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1.7. Probabilistic Setup. We formalize the elements mentioned in the previous sections. Consider a single probability space (Ω , F , P ) supporting several mutually independent random elements including the exogenous burst time τ ∼ ν τ , an infinite sequence of { ι i } i ∈ N i.i.d ∼ ν and Brownian motions { W i } i ∈ N . For N ∈ N , let F N be the P -completed natural filtration of the first N Brownian motions initially enlarged by the ( ι 1 , . . . , ι N ). Let G N denote the progressively enlargement of F N by 1 { t ≤ τ } . Under G , stopping time τ is totally inaccessible 1 . This is important since accessible stopping times could be utilized to create arbitrage opportunities, as noted by Protter [63]. To incorporate varying entry time, consider the product probability space (Ω × [0 , η ] N , F ⊗ B ([0 , η ] N ) , P ⊗ ν N ). Then the sequence of i.i.d entry times {T i } i ∈ N are simply the canonical processes on the respective coordinate.
texts.text: 2.1.7. Probabilistic Setup. We formalize the elements mentioned in the previous sections. Consider a single probability space (Ω , F , P ) supporting several mutually independent random elements including the exogenous burst time τ ∼ ν τ , an infinite sequence of { ι i } i ∈ N i.i.d ∼ ν and Brownian motions { W i } i ∈ N . For N ∈ N , let F N be the P -completed natural filtration of the first N Brownian motions initially enlarged by the ( ι 1 , . . . , ι N ). Let G N denote the progressively enlargement of F N by 1 { t ≤ τ } . Under G , stopping time τ is totally inaccessible 1 . This is important since accessible stopping times could be utilized to create arbitrage opportunities, as noted by Protter [63]. To incorporate varying entry time, consider the product probability space (Ω × [0 , η ] N , F ⊗ B ([0 , η ] N ) , P ⊗ ν N ). Then the sequence of i.i.d entry times {T i } i ∈ N are simply the canonical processes on the respective coordinate.

texts.text: For i = 1 , . . . , N , an admissible strategy α i : [0 , T ] × Ω × [0 , η ] N → A is a P ( G N ) ⊗ B ([0 , η ] N ) -measurable function such that for ν N -almost every t ∈ [0 , η ] N , we have α i ( t ) := α i ( · , · , t ) ∈ A ( t i ; G N ) . Let A i ; G N denote all such strategies.

texts.label: text

texts.orig: Remark 2.7. Our model tacitly assumes that there are noise traders in the market right from the beginning ensuring supply and demand on the asset. Therefore, only the 'sophisticated' investors i ∈ { 1 , . . . , N } influence the growth of the bubble, and we do not impose a net-zero inventory condition. In particular, as often done in the literature on optimal execution (see e.g. [4, 13, 21] and the references therein), the price process is not formed at equilibrium, but rather pre-specified, albeit controlled.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The formulation of the control problem in the N -player game is known as the ' strong' formulation. The associated MFG can be formulated similarly and naturally leads to solving a coupled system of forward-backward stochastic differential equations [16, 17, 18]. While the analysis of such systems is typically quite involved, it would be further complicated in our setting due to the dependence of τ ∗ in µ and the presence of random time τ . Therefore, we choose to adopt the 'weak' formulation of the game that is more suitable in the present case. It is well-known that under mild conditions, strong and weak formulations of stochastic control problems coincide (see e.g. [28, 7, 48]). In the context of MFGs, the weak formulation was first analyzed in the works of Carmona and Lacker [19] and Campi and Fischer [12], from whom we borrow some intuition and ideas. We also refer to Possama¨ ı and Tangpi [62] for more recent developments.

texts.orig: Remark 2.12. Before the representative player enters, both her state X and control α are kept at 0 . At entry, the player has initial state ι which is F 0 -measurable. Note that in the case of fixed entry time, the flow of measures µ of the state in equilibrium should satisfy µ 0 = λ 0 by construction. This is no longer the case as the initial distribution is now 'diluted' by the crowd of traders who have not entered the game and whose controls and states are kept at 0 .

This is no longer the case as the initial distribution is now 'diluted' by the crowd of traders who have not entered the game and whose controls and states are kept at 0 .

texts.orig: Remark 2.13. One might argue that an admissible control should not only be G -progressive, but also progressively measurable with respect to the common noise W 0 . Our definition of admissible strategies A ∗ assumes that players do not take into account the common noise. Considering common noise would drastically complicate the setup (see our accompanying paper [70]). This measurability requirement of A ∗ is however not too restrictive. Indeed, since the running cost f , terminal cost g , and the dynamics of X do not depend on the common noise, and additionally the players are risk-neutral, we show in Proposition A.11 that any MFG equilibrium without common noise is a MFG equilibrium with common noise in our special setting. Therefore, it suffices to only consider G -progressive controls. See Section A.3 for a more detailed discussion.

texts.orig: Remark 2.14. Here we assume existence of a density for τ , making D a single jump Cox process. We take this standard assumption from [49]. If τ has density function f τ ( t ) = r t exp( ∫ t 0 -r s ds ) for a function r t non-negative on [0 , ∞ ) , then Assumption (BT1) is satisfied with k t = r t . The burst intensity process ( k t ) t ∈ [0 ,T ] is assumed to be known by the players. It represents the players' prior regarding the occurrence of an exogenous burst.

texts.orig: Before going any further, let us briefly comment on Theorem 2.15 and its assumptions. Being an MFG equilibrium implies that the optimal control ˆ α is P ( G ) ⊗B [0 , η ]-measurable. Adapting existing fixed point arguments to the present setting allows to obtain a MFG equilibrium ˆ α in the form of a G -predictable process. However, the filtration G is not a model input. Equation (19) allows us to decompose ˆ α into two parts: before the burst occurs, the player follows the strategy ˆ α + ; after the burst, she uses ˆ α -( · ) which depends on the burst time. Both strategies are based only on the market information, i.e. the filtration F . Regarding the model assumptions, (MA1) is typically assumed in MFGs, see e.g. [19, 12]. (MA2) includes mild regularity conditions on the model inputs, and (MA3) is assumed to simplify the exposition. Assumption (BT1) is discussed in Remark 2.14. In addition, we assume that there exist initial players who enter at time 0, who represent the force required to trigger the displacement phase of the bubble.

texts.orig: Remark 2.16. Regarding the link between the N -player game described in Section 2.1 and the MFG, it is well known that MFG equilibria in Theorem 2.15 approximates the weak formulation of the N-player game (see e.g. [19, Theorem 4.2]). In particular, a MFG equilibrium can be used to construct a so-called ε -approximate Nash equilibrium. We provide further details in Section 6.3.
texts.text: Remark 2.16. Regarding the link between the N -player game described in Section 2.1 and the MFG, it is well known that MFG equilibria in Theorem 2.15 approximates the weak formulation of the N-player game (see e.g. [19, Theorem 4.2]). In particular, a MFG equilibrium can be used to construct a so-called ε -approximate Nash equilibrium. We provide further details in Section 6.3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the MFG considered in this paper appears not to have been studied in the literature so far (both for fixed and varying entry times), we propose to analyze it in a general framework. The proof of Theorem 2.15 is given in Section 6 by applying Theorem 5.3, the generalized result. The techniques developed below can undoubtedly apply to other examples of games where players enter the game at varying times or are subject to an unpredictable random shock. To better illustrate the interplay between the equilibrium strategies and the two types of bursts, we now provide some numerical observations on the fixed entry time equilibrium liquidation strategy under different settings.

texts.orig: Anticipating a more detailed discussion on the simulation results in Section 6.2, notice that the presence of the bubble slows down the trading speed initially, as players attempt to ride the bubble first and collectively attack it later, which can be observed through the increasing selling speed before the endogenous burst (see endogenous burst sample path Figure 1). This bubble riding behavior leads to a concave-shaped inventory, in contrast to a convex inventory curve found in standard optimal execution MFGs (see [13, Figure 3], [21, Figure 6.2] or our Figure 2b below). On the other hand, when an early exogenous burst occurs, in which case the traders are caught off guard, we see a sudden increase in selling speed to avoid terminal penalty (see exogenous burst sample path in Figure 1). It turns out that the level of fear among the traders towards the likelihood

texts.orig: Note that G is the smallest filtration which contains F and such that τ is a G -stopping time. Since F is just the P -completed Brownian filtration, by martingale representation theorem we have that any F -martingale is continuous. By [6], random time τ is a G -totally inaccessible stopping time. Since we assume that τ is independent from W and ι , it follows from [18, Proposition 1.3] that F is immersed in G . In other words, the so-called (H)-hypothesis is satisfied: any square integrable ( F , P )-martingale is a square integrable ( G , P )-martingale. In particular, W remains a ( G , P )-Wiener process by L´ evy's characterization theorem. From now on, we work with the P -completed filtered probability space (Ω , F , G , P ). Consequently, the unique control in (C3) does not explicitly depend on ¯ τ or τ . Together with the separability property in (C1), we implicitly require that ˆ a only depends on t, x and z . Assumptions (SD1) and (C1) guarantee that the minimized Hamiltonian is always attained by ˆ a ( t, x , z ) . We hence get the decomposition of h as h ( t, x , q, ¯ τ, τ, z ) = h 0 ( t, x , q, ¯ τ, z ) 1 { 0 ≤ t<τ } + h 1 ( t, x , q, z ) 1 { τ ≤ t } with

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Existence Result for Fixed Entry Time: Proof of Theorem 3.2
texts.text: 4. Existence Result for Fixed Entry Time: Proof of Theorem 3.2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Throughout this section, we assume (SD), (BT), (C) and that T = 0. The method used to prove Theorem 3.2 is based on BSDE arguments as customary in probabilistic approaches to stochastic control problems. In the present case, we analyze the BSDEs on the enlarged filtration G . Therefore, it would be helpful to review relevant results of BSDE theory under progressive enlargement of filtration, notably well-posedness, comparison and a representation of solutions with respect to equations written in the small filtration F . These results are slight extensions of results from Kharroubi and Lim [49]. We provide the proofs in the appendix for completeness.
texts.text: Throughout this section, we assume (SD), (BT), (C) and that T = 0. The method used to prove Theorem 3.2 is based on BSDE arguments as customary in probabilistic approaches to stochastic control problems. In the present case, we analyze the BSDEs on the enlarged filtration G . Therefore, it would be helpful to review relevant results of BSDE theory under progressive enlargement of filtration, notably well-posedness, comparison and a representation of solutions with respect to equations written in the small filtration F . These results are slight extensions of results from Kharroubi and Lim [49]. We provide the proofs in the appendix for completeness.

texts.orig: Remark 4.7. For the control processes, a natural choice of spaces to work with would be Θ defined in (14). However, the control processes do not have the required regularity for us to directly consider measures on A . It turns out to be more favorable if we look at P ([0 , T ] ×P ( A )) instead. We are now considering the space of relaxed controls which contains the standard controls as Dirac measures. Therefore, a MFG equilibrium in Definition 2.11 can be equivalently stated in terms of this larger space as well. It is worth noting that by considering relaxed controls, Campi and Fischer [12] proved that M 0 K is indeed compact.

texts.orig: Lemma 4.8. The unique minimizer ˆ a of the Hamiltonian in (C3) satisfies ˆ a t = ˆ a ( t, x , z ) for a function ˆ a that is jointly measurable in all variables. For each ( t, x ) ∈ [0 , T ] × X , the function z ↦→ ˆ a ( t, x , z ) is continuous in z . Moreover, the mapping P ( A ) × R ∋ ( q, z ) ↦→ h ( t, x , q, ¯ τ, τ, z ) is also continuous for fixed ¯ τ, τ ∈ [0 , T ] × R + .
texts.text: Lemma 4.8. The unique minimizer ˆ a of the Hamiltonian in (C3) satisfies ˆ a t = ˆ a ( t, x , z ) for a function ˆ a that is jointly measurable in all variables. For each ( t, x ) ∈ [0 , T ] × X , the function z ↦→ ˆ a ( t, x , z ) is continuous in z . Moreover, the mapping P ( A ) × R ∋ ( q, z ) ↦→ h ( t, x , q, ¯ τ, τ, z ) is also continuous for fixed ¯ τ, τ ∈ [0 , T ] × R + .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. The separability condition in (C1) ensures that the unique minimizer ˆ α is a function of ( t, x , z ). The continuity conditions in (C1) ensure that for each ( t, x ), the Hamiltonian is jointly continuous in ( q, z, a ). Since A is compact, we can apply Berge's maximum theorem (e.g. [3, Theorem 17.31]) to conclude that for each ( t, x , ¯ τ, τ ), ˆ a ( t, x , · ) is continuous in z , and h ( t, x , · , ¯ τ, τ, · ) is continuous. In particular, ˆ a is a Carath´ eodory function, which is jointly measurable [3, Lemma 4.51]. □

In particular, ˆ a is a Carath´ eodory function, which is jointly measurable [3, Lemma 4.51]. □

texts.orig: Theorem 4.10 (Brouwer-Schauder-Tychonoff) . Let K be a non-empty compact convex subset of a locally convex Hausdorff space, and let Ψ : K → K be a continuous function. Then the set of fixed points of Ψ is compact and nonempty.
texts.text: Theorem 4.10 (Brouwer-Schauder-Tychonoff) . Let K be a non-empty compact convex subset of a locally convex Hausdorff space, and let Ψ : K → K be a continuous function. Then the set of fixed points of Ψ is compact and nonempty.

texts.label: text

texts.orig: Corollary 4.11. Let P be a locally convex metric space and K ⊂ P be convex and relatively compact in P . If Ψ : P → K is continuous, then fixed points of Ψ exist in K .
texts.text: Corollary 4.11. Let P be a locally convex metric space and K ⊂ P be convex and relatively compact in P . If Ψ : P → K is continuous, then fixed points of Ψ exist in K .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let K ⊆ P be the closure of K in P , which by assumption is compact and convex. Let Ψ K be the restriction of Ψ to K . Apply Theorem 4.10 to get a fixed point of Ψ K . Since the mapping only takes values in K , the fixed point has to be in K . Hence it is also a fixed point for Ψ. □

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Existence Result for Varying Entry Times
texts.text: 5. Existence Result for Varying Entry Times

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we look at MFGs with varying entry times. Let us recall that players enter the game in the time frame [0 , η ] independently from each other with law ν . The entry of the players creates a jump in the state process. Therefore, we need to generalize our setup, and then we state and prove the existence result in this setting. The game is still solved on the enlarged filtration G . In other words, when the agent enters the game, she knows whether the burst of the bubble has occurred. We do not force entry before burst, and the model reduces to a standard optimal execution problem on {T ≥ τ ∗ } .
texts.text: In this section, we look at MFGs with varying entry times. Let us recall that players enter the game in the time frame [0 , η ] independently from each other with law ν . The entry of the players creates a jump in the state process. Therefore, we need to generalize our setup, and then we state and prove the existence result in this setting. The game is still solved on the enlarged filtration G . In other words, when the agent enters the game, she knows whether the burst of the bubble has occurred. We do not force entry before burst, and the model reduces to a standard optimal execution problem on {T ≥ τ ∗ } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 5.1. MFG Setup with Varying Entry Times. Let us recall from Section 2.2 that we start with the product probability space (Ω × [0 , η ] , F ⊗ B ([0 , η ]) , P ⊗ ν ) that carries independent variables ι and τ with respective laws λ 0 and ν τ . It also carries an independent Brownian motion W , and F is the filtration generated by the Brownian motion and the initial state, completed by P -null sets. Using the same procedure described in Section 3.1, we progressively enlarge this filtration to G and make the exogenous burst time τ a totally inaccessible stopping time.
texts.text: 5.1. MFG Setup with Varying Entry Times. Let us recall from Section 2.2 that we start with the product probability space (Ω × [0 , η ] , F ⊗ B ([0 , η ]) , P ⊗ ν ) that carries independent variables ι and τ with respective laws λ 0 and ν τ . It also carries an independent Brownian motion W , and F is the filtration generated by the Brownian motion and the initial state, completed by P -null sets. Using the same procedure described in Section 3.1, we progressively enlarge this filtration to G and make the exogenous burst time τ a totally inaccessible stopping time.

texts.orig: Remark 5.1. Let us recall from Lemma 4.8 that the minimizer function ˆ a of the Hamiltonian H in (23) is a measurable function of ( t, x , z ) and continuous in z . If we assume (ET) in addition to Assumptions (C) and (SD) , specifically the continuity of f a in x , then H is jointly continuous in ( x , z ) . Therefore, by Berge's maximum theorem [3, Theorem 17.31], the minimizer function ˆ a is also jointly continuous in ( x , z ) for fixed t ∈ [0 , T ] .
texts.text: Remark 5.1. Let us recall from Lemma 4.8 that the minimizer function ˆ a of the Hamiltonian H in (23) is a measurable function of ( t, x , z ) and continuous in z . If we assume (ET) in addition to Assumptions (C) and (SD) , specifically the continuity of f a in x , then H is jointly continuous in ( x , z ) . Therefore, by Berge's maximum theorem [3, Theorem 17.31], the minimizer function ˆ a is also jointly continuous in ( x , z ) for fixed t ∈ [0 , T ] .

texts.orig: Assumption (BT2) . Let M K be the closure of M K . In light of Lemma 5.6 and Corollary 4.11, we only need to check continuity of ¯ τ ( · ) on M K instead of M . By continuity assumption on F T and Lemma 5.9, all laws in M K have continuous mean trajectory. Taking closure in Wasserstein space preserves the continuity of the mean processes. Take a sequence ( µ n ) n in M K converging in the Wasserstein sense to µ ∈ M K . Let ¯ µ n and ¯ µ be the entry-weighted average processes defined in (15). Since F T (0) > 0, the process ¯ µ n is continuous for each n and converges uniformly to ¯ µ , which is also continuous.

Take a sequence ( µ n ) n in M K converging in the Wasserstein sense to µ ∈ M K . Let ¯ µ n and ¯ µ be the entry-weighted average processes defined in (15). Since F T (0) > 0, the process ¯ µ n is continuous for each n and converges uniformly to ¯ µ , which is also continuous.

texts.orig: Notice that the setup becomes almost linear-quadratic (LQ). It is well-known that explicit solutions to a true linear-quadratic game can be obtained via solving Ricatti ordinary differential equations (e.g. [20, 8]). However, the interaction through endogenous burst time and the presence of a random exogenous burst time prevent us from directly following this traditional route. Some useful observations from the LQ framework such as the form of the solutions can be borrowed nonetheless. We numerically solve the iterative BSDE (58) by discretization and use the implicit scheme described in [59] to take advantage of the LQ structure. 4 Within each iteration we randomly draw an initial inventory ι , a sample path of Brownian motion W and an exogenous burst time τ .
texts.text: Notice that the setup becomes almost linear-quadratic (LQ). It is well-known that explicit solutions to a true linear-quadratic game can be obtained via solving Ricatti ordinary differential equations (e.g. [20, 8]). However, the interaction through endogenous burst time and the presence of a random exogenous burst time prevent us from directly following this traditional route. Some useful observations from the LQ framework such as the form of the solutions can be borrowed nonetheless. We numerically solve the iterative BSDE (58) by discretization and use the implicit scheme described in [59] to take advantage of the LQ structure. 4 Within each iteration we randomly draw an initial inventory ι , a sample path of Brownian motion W and an exogenous burst time τ .

texts.orig: which gives us the bubble until burst. By construction, the equilibrium strategy always jumps at ¯ τ ( µ ) because the equilibrium endogenous burst time is anticipated by all traders. Therefore, a sudden drop in trading speed takes place right after ¯ τ ( µ ) as players shift from profit-taking to standard optimal execution. However, if an early exogenous burst occurs, the players react in the opposite direction and immediately start dumping their shares at a higher pace (see for instance the exogenous burst paths in Figure 1b, 2a and 2c). These reactions are consistent in all scenarios where a bubble is present.

texts.orig: When the growth of the bubble is strong, traders may initially buy more shares to take advantage of the upward trend, which they use to offset the higher liquidation costs (see Figure 2a and 2c). However, an interesting phenomenon occurs in the BigBubble setting where traders collectively delay the endogenous burst, compared to the Default setting, in order to take further advantage of the explosive growth while keeping the transaction costs reasonable. On the contrary, traders in the LowImpact scenario will trigger the burst sooner, even compared to the Default case, by selling faster. In the FearExo setting, we increase the level of concern towards an exogenous shock, parameterized by k > 0. Observe that the profit-taking phase before the exogenous burst is no longer present. Instead, players adopt a more conservative strategy by reducing inventory much earlier in the game.
texts.text: When the growth of the bubble is strong, traders may initially buy more shares to take advantage of the upward trend, which they use to offset the higher liquidation costs (see Figure 2a and 2c). However, an interesting phenomenon occurs in the BigBubble setting where traders collectively delay the endogenous burst, compared to the Default setting, in order to take further advantage of the explosive growth while keeping the transaction costs reasonable. On the contrary, traders in the LowImpact scenario will trigger the burst sooner, even compared to the Default case, by selling faster. In the FearExo setting, we increase the level of concern towards an exogenous shock, parameterized by k > 0. Observe that the profit-taking phase before the exogenous burst is no longer present. Instead, players adopt a more conservative strategy by reducing inventory much earlier in the game.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To better illustrate this change in mindset as the traders' prior on the exogenous burst varies, we fix all parameters to the Default setting and plot the equilibrium strategies at different values of k (see Figure 3). When k is small (i.e. traders expect the exogenous burst to happen far in the future), we see a concave shape in the equilibrium liquidation trajectory which resembles a 'riding before attacking' strategy. When k → 0, players believe that there is almost no risk of an external shock. If strong enough, this optimism alone could trigger players to initially buy shares to take advantage of the bubble. However, as we increase k , the concavity becomes less and less pronounced and traders eventually shift to a pessimistic strategy for which the equilibrium liquidation trajectory becomes convex , resembling a 'risk-aversion' strategy. The traders are thus reluctant to ride the bubble because they consider the bubble as a liability, rather than an opportunity. As k →∞ , the bubble bursts closer to 0 which eventually reduces to the NoBubble scenario. It would be interesting to determine the critical point at which players shift from the optimistic to the pessimistic strategy. Observing the simulated results, it seems that the change of regimes occurs around the value of k at which the equilibrium endogenous burst time is equal to the expected exogenous burst time.
texts.text: To better illustrate this change in mindset as the traders' prior on the exogenous burst varies, we fix all parameters to the Default setting and plot the equilibrium strategies at different values of k (see Figure 3). When k is small (i.e. traders expect the exogenous burst to happen far in the future), we see a concave shape in the equilibrium liquidation trajectory which resembles a 'riding before attacking' strategy. When k → 0, players believe that there is almost no risk of an external shock. If strong enough, this optimism alone could trigger players to initially buy shares to take advantage of the bubble. However, as we increase k , the concavity becomes less and less pronounced and traders eventually shift to a pessimistic strategy for which the equilibrium liquidation trajectory becomes convex , resembling a 'risk-aversion' strategy. The traders are thus reluctant to ride the bubble because they consider the bubble as a liability, rather than an opportunity. As k →∞ , the bubble bursts closer to 0 which eventually reduces to the NoBubble scenario. It would be interesting to determine the critical point at which players shift from the optimistic to the pessimistic strategy. Observing the simulated results, it seems that the change of regimes occurs around the value of k at which the equilibrium endogenous burst time is equal to the expected exogenous burst time.

texts.orig: 6.3. MFG Approximation of Finite-player Games. In this section, we elaborate on the point made in Remark 2.16 that an approximate Nash equilibrium for the finite-player game can be constructed by a MFG equilibrium. Since the result is largely already presented in [19, Sections 4 and 8], we shall only go over the main arguments to avoid repetition.
texts.text: 6.3. MFG Approximation of Finite-player Games. In this section, we elaborate on the point made in Remark 2.16 that an approximate Nash equilibrium for the finite-player game can be constructed by a MFG equilibrium. Since the result is largely already presented in [19, Sections 4 and 8], we shall only go over the main arguments to avoid repetition.

texts.orig: In order to construct an ε -Nash equilibrium, first note that since σ > 0, the process ( ι i , W i t ) 0 ≤ t ≤ T generates the same filtration as X i . Therefore, given a MFG equilibrium (ˆ α, ˆ θ, ˆ µ ) from Theorem 6.1, the open-loop control ˆ α can be written in a closed loop form, namely a deterministic, measurable function φ of ( t, X, T ). We briefly outline the proof to show that for each ε > 0, there exists M ε ∈ N such that for all N ≥ M ε , the strategy profile ˆ α = (ˆ α 1 , . . . , ˆ α N ) is an ε -Nash equilibrium for the N -player game where ˆ α i t = φ ( t, X i , T i ). In other words, we can construct a strategy profile where each player only uses her own information, that is X i and T i , such that it can approximate a true Nash equilibrium arbitrarily well in the sense of Definition 6.3. We only need to show the inequality (61) for the first player by symmetry.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Concluding Remarks
texts.text: 7. Concluding Remarks

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we propose a model on optimal bubble riding under a stochastic differential game setting, in which traders realize the presence of the bubble at possibly different times before they enter the market and begin liquidating their assets. Each trader attempts to take advantage of the uptrend knowing that a future crash will happen. This formulation leads to a MFG with varying entry times, where players interact through both permanent price impact and the endogenous burst condition. We then prove the existence of MFG equilibria in a generic framework by a fixed point argument. To incorporate the exogenous burst, we adopt the techniques of progressive enlargement of filtration from credit risk literature, which enables us to decompose the MFG equilibrium into pre-burst and post-burst components that only require market information and time of exogenous burst once it occurs.
texts.text: In this paper, we propose a model on optimal bubble riding under a stochastic differential game setting, in which traders realize the presence of the bubble at possibly different times before they enter the market and begin liquidating their assets. Each trader attempts to take advantage of the uptrend knowing that a future crash will happen. This formulation leads to a MFG with varying entry times, where players interact through both permanent price impact and the endogenous burst condition. We then prove the existence of MFG equilibria in a generic framework by a fixed point argument. To incorporate the exogenous burst, we adopt the techniques of progressive enlargement of filtration from credit risk literature, which enables us to decompose the MFG equilibrium into pre-burst and post-burst components that only require market information and time of exogenous burst once it occurs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We revisit the model with simulation results by numerically solving the BSDE in the weak formulation of MFGs. The equilibrium control is found to have a different shape from the classical optimal execution problems, showing both a riding phase and an attacking phase in the liquidation strategy. While the uptrend provides motivation for herding strategies, the inevitable, random burst time propels the traders to eventually attack the bubble. We also notice that traders could collectively delay the endogenous burst in the presence of a strong enough bubble.
texts.text: We revisit the model with simulation results by numerically solving the BSDE in the weak formulation of MFGs. The equilibrium control is found to have a different shape from the classical optimal execution problems, showing both a riding phase and an attacking phase in the liquidation strategy. While the uptrend provides motivation for herding strategies, the inevitable, random burst time propels the traders to eventually attack the bubble. We also notice that traders could collectively delay the endogenous burst in the presence of a strong enough bubble.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another intriguing result arises when we analyze how traders' view on the exogenous burst influences their equilibrium strategy. As the traders expect an earlier exogenous burst, there is a critical point where the concave shaped liquidation strategies become convex. It would be interesting to further investigate this regime change. Lastly, we give a proof outline of the inverse convergence result, showing that the MFG equilibrium can be used to construct an approximate Nash equilibrium. As we are the first to look at MFGs with varying entry times, there are many future directions to take. Some examples include having players choose their entry time based on their risk preference, allowing inference on k , or incorporating a leader-follower framework to model both institutional and retail traders. We also refer the readers to the accompanying paper [70] for an extension with price-dependent entry times and common noise.
texts.text: Another intriguing result arises when we analyze how traders' view on the exogenous burst influences their equilibrium strategy. As the traders expect an earlier exogenous burst, there is a critical point where the concave shaped liquidation strategies become convex. It would be interesting to further investigate this regime change. Lastly, we give a proof outline of the inverse convergence result, showing that the MFG equilibrium can be used to construct an approximate Nash equilibrium. As we are the first to look at MFGs with varying entry times, there are many future directions to take. Some examples include having players choose their entry time based on their risk preference, allowing inference on k , or incorporating a leader-follower framework to model both institutional and retail traders. We also refer the readers to the accompanying paper [70] for an extension with price-dependent entry times and common noise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The appendix consists of three sections. We first present the proofs of results on BSDEs under enlarged filtration used in the proof of our MFG existence results. These follow by adaptations of Kharroubi and Lim [49]. Then we provide some regularity results needed for the varying entry times section, as well as a brief discussion on common noise.
texts.text: The appendix consists of three sections. We first present the proofs of results on BSDEs under enlarged filtration used in the proof of our MFG existence results. These follow by adaptations of Kharroubi and Lim [49]. Then we provide some regularity results needed for the varying entry times section, as well as a brief discussion on common noise.

texts.orig: Remark A.3. In [49], the authors make the assumption that Y i is an essentially bounded process (on [0 , T ] , i = 0 , 1 ) for a stronger conclusion that Y is also essentially bounded. Since we do not have a bounded terminal cost, we obtain an integrability property of the solution instead of boundedness. The proof that ( Y, Z, U ) is in S 2 G ×H 2 G ×H 2 G ,D easily follows from the original proof, thus we omit it here.

texts.orig: Lemma A.8. The space ( X ∗ , d X ∗ ) defined in Section 2.2 is a separable and complete metric space.
texts.text: Lemma A.8. The space ( X ∗ , d X ∗ ) defined in Section 2.2 is a separable and complete metric space.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. It is easy to check that d X ∗ is indeed a metric. Separability follows from that of X . For completeness, take a Cauchy sequence { x n ( t n ) } n ≥ 1 in X ∗ . Then by definition of d X ∗ , the sequence of entry times t n must be Cauchy and therefore converges to some t ∗ ∈ [0 , η ]. Similarly, the continuous counterparts ¯ x n ( t n ) is a Cauchy sequence in X and thus converges to some ¯ x ∗ ∈ X . In particular, ¯ x ∗ must be constant on [0 , t ∗ ]. Then it is not hard to show that the Cauchy sequence x n ( t n ) converges to x ∗ ( t ∗ ) ∈ X ∗ with respect to the metric d X ∗ , where x ∗ t ( t ∗ ) := 1 { t ≥ t ∗ } ¯ x ∗ t . □
texts.text: Proof. It is easy to check that d X ∗ is indeed a metric. Separability follows from that of X . For completeness, take a Cauchy sequence { x n ( t n ) } n ≥ 1 in X ∗ . texts.label: text
texts.orig: A.3. MFGs with Common Noise. Let us recall that the filtration that we work with in this paper does not include the noise term driving the price dynamics W 0 , but only the individual inventory information W . In this section we elaborate on Remark 2.13 to justify this seemingly restrictive assumption. Unlike the idiosyncratic noise W , the common noise remains even when N → ∞ . As a consequence, θ and µ become random laws which require a different definition for MFG equilibrium. In particular, they now become the conditional laws of controls and state given W 0 .
texts.text: A.3. MFGs with Common Noise. Let us recall that the filtration that we work with in this paper does not include the noise term driving the price dynamics W 0 , but only the individual inventory information W . In this section we elaborate on Remark 2.13 to justify this seemingly restrictive assumption. Unlike the idiosyncratic noise W , the common noise remains even when N → ∞ . As a consequence, θ and µ become random laws which require a different definition for MFG equilibrium. In particular, they now become the conditional laws of controls and state given W 0 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let (Ω , F , P ) also carry another Wiener process W 0 that is independent from everything else. Denote by F c := ( F c t ) t ∈ [0 ,T ] the natural filtration now including common noise and by G c its progressive enlargement by τ , where F c t is the P -completion of σ (( ι, W s , W 0 s ) s ∈ [0 ,t ] ). Also define F W 0 := ( F W 0 t ) t ∈ [0 ,T ] to be the natural filtration generated by W 0 . Let A ∗ ,c ( t ∗ ) and A ∗ ,c follow Definition 2.9 but with common noise (i.e. G c -progressive, A-valued processes with appropriate entry times).

Assumption (C3), Remark 3.1 and Proposition 4.3 imply that ˆ α t ( t ∗ ) = ˆ a ( t, X t ∗ , Z t ∗ t ) for almost every t . Now consider the following BSDE on [ t ∗ , T ] solved on the filtration G c :

Now consider the following BSDE on [ t ∗ , T ] solved on the filtration G c :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Y t ∗ ,c t = g ( X t ∗ , ¯ τ ( µ c ) , τ ) + ∫ T t h ( s, X t ∗ , θ c s , ¯ τ ( µ ) , τ, Z t ∗ ,c s ) ds -∫ T t Z t ∗ ,c s dW s -∫ T t Z t ∗ ,c s dW 0 s -∫ T t U t ∗ ,c s dM s . (70)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Asolution to this BSDE is ( Y t ∗ ,c , Z t ∗ ,c , Z t ∗ ,c , U t ∗ ,c ) ∈ S 2 G c [ t ∗ , T ] ×H 2 G c [ t ∗ , T ] ×H 2 G c [ t ∗ , T ] ×H 2 G c ,D [ t ∗ , T ], which exists and is unique (see e.g. [31, Theorem 53.1]). However, it is also easy to verify that ( Y t ∗ , Z t ∗ , 0 , U t ∗ ) is a solution by construction, so by uniqueness, ( Y t ∗ ,c , Z t ∗ ,c ) = ( Y t ∗ , Z t ∗ ) and ˆ α t ( t ∗ ) = ˆ a ( t, X t ∗ , Z t ∗ ,c t ) for t ∈ [ t ∗ , T ] which is still the unique minimizer of the Hamiltonian. Thus, using comparison principle of BSDE (70), for any β ∈ A ∗ ,c ( t ∗ ) we have
texts.text: Asolution to this BSDE is ( Y t ∗ ,c , Z t ∗ ,c , Z t ∗ ,c , U t ∗ ,c ) ∈ S 2 G c [ t ∗ , T ] ×H 2 G c [ t ∗ , T ] ×H 2 G c [ t ∗ , T ] ×H 2 G c ,D [ t ∗ , T ], which exists and is unique (see e.g. [31, Theorem 53.1]). However, it is also easy to verify that ( Y t ∗ , Z t ∗ , 0 , U t ∗ ) is a solution by construction, so by uniqueness, ( Y t ∗ ,c , Z t ∗ ,c ) = ( Y t ∗ , Z t ∗ ) and ˆ α t ( t ∗ ) = ˆ a ( t, X t ∗ , Z t ∗ ,c t ) for t ∈ [ t ∗ , T ] which is still the unique minimizer of the Hamiltonian. texts.label: formula
texts.orig: J θ,µ,t ∗ ( β ) = E P β [ g ( X t ∗ , ¯ τ ( µ ) , τ ) + ∫ T t ∗ f ( s, X t ∗ , θ s , ¯ τ ( µ ) , τ, β ) ds ] ≤ E [ Y t ∗ ,c t ∗ ] = E P ˆ α ( t ∗ ) [ Y t ∗ ,c t ∗ ] = J θ,µ,t ∗ (ˆ α ( t ∗ )) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have checked that (ˆ α, θ, µ ) satisfies both the optimality condition and the fixed point condition, so it is a MFG equilibrium with common noise. □
texts.text: We have checked that (ˆ α, θ, µ ) satisfies both the optimality condition and the fixed point condition, so it is a MFG equilibrium with common noise. □

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The argument above implies the existence, albeit a somewhat trivial one, of MFG equilibrium even if we allow our controls to depend on common noise. However, it is important to note that the key reason enabling our model to avoid common noise is that the bubble trend functions depends only on the entry time, which is specified exogenously. Proposition A.11 would not be applicable for instance if individual entry also depended on the price trajectory. See our accompanying paper [70] for a full treatment.
